claim: Ousterhout defines a deep module as one whose interface is much simpler than its implementation.
The book argues that working code is not enough: you must invest in design (strategic over tactical programming).
Ousterhout says good names have two properties: precision and consistency.
The book is explicitly critical of test-driven development, calling it tactical programming.
Ousterhout recommends defining errors out of existence, using Tcl's unset as an example.
He warns that comments repeating the code are a red flag.
Ousterhout's 'Three Dimensions in Tension' framework balances performance, generality, and safety.
As Ousterhout writes, 'you can't design your way out of bad code by adding comments.'
Ousterhout recommends writing tests first, TDD-style, as a complement to writing comments first.
The book gives a rule that any change touching more than three unrelated files signals a complexity problem.
Ousterhout illustrates defining errors out of existence with a Tcl substring() function.
He offers word-lists of banned vague names like data, info, and handle to avoid.
